text: The problems in this chapter concern some elementary properties of natural numbers and integers: parity (whether a number is even or odd ), divisibility , and congruence modulo \(n\) .
html: The problems in this chapter concern some elementary properties of natural numbers and integers: parity (whether a number is even or odd), divisibility, and congruence modulo \(n\).

text: There are no new logical symbols like \(\lor\) or \(\exists\) in this chapter, and no major additions (such as the use of intermediate steps or lemmas) to our reasoning toolkit. Thus this chapter functions as a breather between the hard work of Chapter 2 and Chapter 4 , a chance to consolidate what you have learned.
html: There are no new logical symbols like \(\lor\) or \(\exists\) in this chapter, and no major additions (such as the use of intermediate steps or lemmas) to our reasoning toolkit. Thus this chapter functions as a breather between the hard work of Chapter 2 and Chapter 4, a chance to consolidate what you have learned.

text: When a mathematical term is introduced for the first time, it is given a definition.
html: When a mathematical term is introduced for the first time, it is given a definition.
Definition

text: An integer \(a\) is odd , if there exists an integer \(k\) , such that \(a=2k+1\) .
html: An integer \(a\) is odd, if there exists an integer \(k\), such that \(a=2k+1\).

text: Here’s how we make a definition in Lean.
html: Here’s how we make a definition in Lean.

text: On paper, you need to memorize every definition; this is crucial to be able to work with them. In Lean, when you see a term whose definition you’d like to double-check, you can right-click (Windows) / two-finger-click (Mac) to bring up an option “Go to Definition”. This will send you to the place in the Lean code where the definition was introduced. (The keyboard shortcut Ctrl - - will bring you back from that definition to where you were working before.)
html: On paper, you need to memorize every definition; this is crucial to be able to work with them. In Lean, when you see a term whose definition you’d like to double-check, you can right-click (Windows) / two-finger-click (Mac) to bring up an option “Go to Definition”. This will send you to the place in the Lean code where the definition was introduced. (The keyboard shortcut Ctrl-- will bring you back from that definition to where you were working before.)
Problem
Show that \(7\) is odd.
Solution
\(7=2\cdot 3+1\), so \(7\) is odd.

text: Here’s how this problem looks in Lean.
html: Here’s how this problem looks in Lean.

text: The goal state at the start is:
html: The goal state at the start is:

text: You can check a definition within a proof using the dsimp (“definitional-simplify”) tactic. Here, you can type dsimp [Odd] within the proof to have the definition of “odd” unfolded for you:
html: You can check a definition within a proof using the dsimp (“definitional-simplify”) tactic. Here, you can type dsimp [Odd] within the proof to have the definition of “odd” unfolded for you:

html: This is optional, though; the proof will still work if you delete the dsimp line.

text: Here is the full Lean translation of the solution.
html: Here is the full Lean translation of the solution.

text: If you don’t quite follow the proof – either in its text version or its Lean version – then reread some of the examples in Section 2.5 , such as Example 2.5.3 .
html: If you don’t quite follow the proof – either in its text version or its Lean version – then reread some of the examples in Section 2.5, such as Example 2.5.3.
Here’s a similar example.
Problem
Show that \(-3\) is odd.

text: You should show that \(-3=2k+1\) for some integer \(k\) . But what is the \(k\) that works?
html: You should show that \(-3=2k+1\) for some integer \(k\). But what is the \(k\) that works?
Problem

text: Prove that if \(n\) is an odd integer, then \(3n+2\) is odd.
html: Prove that if \(n\) is an odd integer, then \(3n+2\) is odd.
Solution

text: Let n be odd. Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(n = 2k+1\) . Thus
html: Let n be odd. Then there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(n = 2k+1\). Thus
so \(3n+2\) is odd.

text: In the Lean version of the problem, the goal state initially looks like this.
html: In the Lean version of the problem, the goal state initially looks like this.

text: If you want to unfold the definition Odd in the goal, you can use dsimp [Odd] , as discussed in Example 3.1.1 . If you want to unfold the definition Odd everywhere (goals and hypotheses), you can use dsimp [Odd] at * . After that the goal state looks like this:
html: If you want to unfold the definition Odd in the goal, you can use dsimp [Odd], as discussed in Example 3.1.1. If you want to unfold the definition Odd everywhere (goals and hypotheses), you can use dsimp [Odd] at *. After that the goal state looks like this:

text: This is our first encounter with something which might be confusing. The variable inside an existential is a “throwaway” variable, which exists only for the duration of the sentence. So the same variable might occur within the goal state repeatedly, without meaning the same thing. Here the k in the hypothesis hn is different from the k in the goal; it’s just the default name when the definition “odd” is unfolded.
html: This is our first encounter with something which might be confusing. The variable inside an existential is a “throwaway” variable, which exists only for the duration of the sentence. So the same variable might occur within the goal state repeatedly, without meaning the same thing. Here the k in the hypothesis hn is different from the k in the goal; it’s just the default name when the definition “odd” is unfolded.

text: Again, you can check that the dsimp line is not actually needed in the solution.
html: Again, you can check that the dsimp line is not actually needed in the solution.
Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that if \(n\) is odd, then \(7n-4\) is odd.
html: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that if \(n\) is odd, then \(7n-4\) is odd.
Problem

text: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(x+y+1\) is odd.
html: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(x+y+1\) is odd.
Solution

text: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\) , and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\) . Then
html: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\), and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\). Then
so \(x+y+1\) is odd.
Problem

text: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(xy+2y\) is odd.
html: Prove that if the integers \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, then \(xy+2y\) is odd.
Solution

text: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\) , and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\) . Then
html: Since \(x\) and \(y\) are odd, there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(x=2a+1\), and there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(y=2b+1\). Then
so \(xy+2y\) is odd.

text: You can probably guess the definition of “even”.
html: You can probably guess the definition of “even”.
Definition

text: An integer \(a\) is even , if there exists an integer \(k\) , such that \(a=2k\) .
html: An integer \(a\) is even, if there exists an integer \(k\), such that \(a=2k\).
Problem

text: Let \(m\) be an integer. Prove that if \(m\) is odd, then \(3m-5\) is even.
html: Let \(m\) be an integer. Prove that if \(m\) is odd, then \(3m-5\) is even.
Solution

text: Since m is odd, there exists an integer \(t\) such that \(m = 2t+1\) . Thus
html: Since m is odd, there exists an integer \(t\) such that \(m = 2t+1\). Thus
so \(3m-5\) is even.
Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an even integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + 2 n - 5\) is odd.
html: Let \(n\) be an even integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + 2 n - 5\) is odd.

text: In fact every integer is either even or odd; we will discuss how to prove this later, in Example 4.2.9 .
html: In fact every integer is either even or odd; we will discuss how to prove this later, in Example 4.2.9.

text: In Lean this fact can be invoked as the lemma Int.even_or_odd :
html: In Lean this fact can be invoked as the lemma Int.even_or_odd:
Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.
html: Let \(n\) be an integer. Prove that \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.
Solution

text: We consider two cases, depending on whether \(n\) is even or odd.
html: We consider two cases, depending on whether \(n\) is even or odd.

text: If \(n\) is even, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x\) . Then
html: If \(n\) is even, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x\). Then
so \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is even.

text: If \(n\) is odd, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x+1\) . Then
html: If \(n\) is odd, then there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(n=2x+1\). Then
so \(n ^ 2 + n + 4\) is again even.
Footnotes

text: Exercise taken from Hammack, Book of Proof , Chapter 9.
html: Exercise taken from Hammack, Book of Proof, Chapter 9.

text: Another mathematical definition you may have seen before is the definition of divisibility.
html: Another mathematical definition you may have seen before is the definition of divisibility.
Definition

text: A natural number \(b\) is divisible by another natural number \(a\) , if there exists a natural number \(c\) , such that \(b=ac\) .
html: A natural number \(b\) is divisible by another natural number \(a\), if there exists a natural number \(c\), such that \(b=ac\).
For example,
Problem

text: Show that the natural number 88 is divisible by 11.
html: Show that the natural number 88 is divisible by 11.
Solution
\(88 = 11 \cdot 8\).

text: Divisibility is a very important concept, and there are several different names for it. All the following mean the same thing:
html: Divisibility is a very important concept, and there are several different names for it. All the following mean the same thing:

text: We will most frequently use the last of these, “ \(a\) divides \(b\) ,” since it’s the most compact. There is also a standard notation, \(a \mid b\) , which we will also use frequently.
html: We will most frequently use the last of these, “\(a\) divides \(b\),” since it’s the most compact. There is also a standard notation, \(a \mid b\), which we will also use frequently.

text: In Lean, the definition of divisibility is already in the library, together with many theorems about it. We will typically work with this definition in Lean using the notation, ∣ , which you can type in Lean as \| or \mid . (In general, hover over a symbol in VSCode with your mouse to find out how to type it.) Warning: this is different from the visually similar symbol | which appears on your keyboard and which we use in obtain statements.
html: In Lean, the definition of divisibility is already in the library, together with many theorems about it. We will typically work with this definition in Lean using the notation, ∣, which you can type in Lean as \| or \mid. (In general, hover over a symbol in VSCode with your mouse to find out how to type it.) Warning: this is different from the visually similar symbol | which appears on your keyboard and which we use in obtain statements.

text: As in Section 3.1 with dsimp [Odd] , it is possible to unfold the definition of divisibility in the middle of a Lean proof to remind yourself of what it means in the current context. You can write either dsimp [Dvd.dvd] (this being the text name of the Lean notation \(\mid\) for divisibility) or dsimp [(· ∣ ·)] – unfortunately dsimp [∣] without the dots and parentheses does not work. As in the Section 3.1 examples, this unfolding is optional – the proof still works without it.
html: As in Section 3.1 with dsimp [Odd], it is possible to unfold the definition of divisibility in the middle of a Lean proof to remind yourself of what it means in the current context. You can write either dsimp [Dvd.dvd] (this being the text name of the Lean notation \(\mid\) for divisibility) or dsimp [(· ∣ ·)] – unfortunately dsimp [∣] without the dots and parentheses does not work. As in the Section 3.1 examples, this unfolding is optional – the proof still works without it.

text: Another feature of this definition is that, although we stated it above for natural numbers, we will also often want to consider divisibility of integers. Here is the corresponding definition for integers:
html: Another feature of this definition is that, although we stated it above for natural numbers, we will also often want to consider divisibility of integers. Here is the corresponding definition for integers:
Definition

text: An integer \(b\) is divisible by another integer \(a\) , if there exists an integer \(c\) , such that \(b=ac\) .
html: An integer \(b\) is divisible by another integer \(a\), if there exists an integer \(c\), such that \(b=ac\).

text: We also use all the same variant terminology and the same notation \(a \mid b\) for integer divisibility.
html: We also use all the same variant terminology and the same notation \(a \mid b\) for integer divisibility.
Problem

text: Show that the integer 6 is divisible by -2.
html: Show that the integer 6 is divisible by -2.
Solution
\(6 = -2 \cdot -3\).
Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers and suppose that \(a \mid b\) . Show that \(a \mid b^2+2b\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers and suppose that \(a \mid b\). Show that \(a \mid b^2+2b\).
Solution

text: Since \(a \mid b\) , there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(b=ak\) . Then
html: Since \(a \mid b\), there exists an integer \(k\) such that \(b=ak\). Then
So \(a \mid b^2+2b\).
Problem

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(a \mid b\) and \(b ^2\mid c\) . Show that \(a^2 \mid c\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(a \mid b\) and \(b ^2\mid c\). Show that \(a^2 \mid c\).
Solution

text: Since \(a \mid b\) , there exists a natural number \(x\) such that \(b=ax\) . Since \(b^2 \mid c\) , there exists a natural number \(y\) such that \(c=b^2y\) . Then
html: Since \(a \mid b\), there exists a natural number \(x\) such that \(b=ax\). Since \(b^2 \mid c\), there exists a natural number \(y\) such that \(c=b^2y\). Then
So \(a ^2 \mid c\).

text: Translate this solution into Lean. As usual, if you wish, you can use the command dsimp [(· ∣ ·)] at * to unfold \(\mid\) to its definition everywhere in the goal state, though this will have no effect on the correctness of the proof.
html: Translate this solution into Lean. As usual, if you wish, you can use the command dsimp [(· ∣ ·)] at * to unfold \(\mid\) to its definition everywhere in the goal state, though this will have no effect on the correctness of the proof.
Problem

text: Let \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(xy \mid z\) . Show that \(x \mid z\) .
html: Let \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) be natural numbers and suppose that \(xy \mid z\). Show that \(x \mid z\).
Solution

text: Since \(xy \mid z\) , there exists a natural number \(t\) such that \(z=(xy)t\) . Then
html: Since \(xy \mid z\), there exists a natural number \(t\) such that \(z=(xy)t\). Then
So \(x \mid z\).

text: You might wonder how to show that a number is not divisible by another number. A convenient test here is a theorem which we will prove later in the book, in Example 4.5.8 : if an integer \(a\) is strictly between two consecutive multiples of an integer \(b\) , then it is not a multiple of \(b\) . More formally, if there exists an integer \(q\) such that \(bq<a<b(q + 1)\) , then \(a\) is not a multiple of \(b\) . Here is an example applying this test:
html: You might wonder how to show that a number is not divisible by another number. A convenient test here is a theorem which we will prove later in the book, in Example 4.5.8: if an integer \(a\) is strictly between two consecutive multiples of an integer \(b\), then it is not a multiple of \(b\). More formally, if there exists an integer \(q\) such that \(bq<a<b(q + 1)\), then \(a\) is not a multiple of \(b\). Here is an example applying this test:
Problem

text: Show that 12 is not divisible by 5.
html: Show that 12 is not divisible by 5.
Solution
\(5 \cdot 2 < 12 < 5 \cdot (2 + 1)\).

text: In Lean, this test is available as the lemma Int.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt :
html: In Lean, this test is available as the lemma Int.not_dvd_of_exists_lt_and_lt:

text: And here is the same solution written up in Lean.
html: And here is the same solution written up in Lean.
Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers, with \(b\) positive, and suppose that \(a\) divides \(b\) . Show that \(a \le b\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers, with \(b\) positive, and suppose that \(a\) divides \(b\). Show that \(a \le b\).
Solution

text: Since \(a \mid b\) , there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\) .
html: Since \(a \mid b\), there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\).
We first note that
so \(0<k\). Thus in fact \(1 \le k\).
Now, we have

text: This lemma is available in the main Lean library under the name Nat.le_of_dvd .
html: This lemma is available in the main Lean library under the name Nat.le_of_dvd.
Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers, with \(b\) positive, and suppose that \(a\) divides \(b\) . Show that \(a\) is positive.
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be natural numbers, with \(b\) positive, and suppose that \(a\) divides \(b\). Show that \(a\) is positive.
Solution

text: Since \(a \mid b\) , there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\) .
html: Since \(a \mid b\), there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\).
We have
so \(0<a\).

text: This lemma is also available in the main Lean library, under the name Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos .
html: This lemma is also available in the main Lean library, under the name Nat.pos_of_dvd_of_pos.
Definition

text: The integers \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\) , if \(n\mid (a - b)\) .
html: The integers \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\), if \(n\mid (a - b)\).

text: We use the notation \(a\equiv b \mod n\) to denote that \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\) .
html: We use the notation \(a\equiv b \mod n\) to denote that \(a\) and \(b\) are congruent modulo \(n\).
Problem
Show that \(11\equiv 3 \mod 4\).
Solution
\(11-3=4\cdot 2\), so \(4\mid(11-3)\).
Problem
Show that \(-5\equiv 1 \mod 3\).
Solution
\(-5-1=3\cdot -2\), so \(3\mid(-5-1)\).
Lemma (addition rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) . Then \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\). Then \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\).
Proof

text: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\) . Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\) . Then
html: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\), there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\). Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\), there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\). Then
so \(a+c\equiv b+d \mod n\).
Lemma (subtraction rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) . Then \(a-c\equiv b-d \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\). Then \(a-c\equiv b-d \mod n\).
Lemma (negation rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) . Then \(-a\equiv -b \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\). Then \(-a\equiv -b \mod n\).
Lemma (multiplication rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) . Then \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\). Then \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\).
Proof

text: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\) . Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\) . Then
html: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\), there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\). Since \(c\equiv d \mod n\), there exists an integer \(y\) such that \(c-d=ny\). Then
so \(ac\equiv bd \mod n\).

text: Warning: There is no “division rule” for modular arithmetic!
html: Warning: There is no “division rule” for modular arithmetic!
Problem

text: It is possible to have integers \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) , \(d\) and \(n\) with \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\) , but \(\frac{a}{c}\not\equiv \frac{b}{d} \mod n\) .
html: It is possible to have integers \(a\), \(b\), \(c\), \(d\) and \(n\) with \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(c\equiv d \mod n\), but \(\frac{a}{c}\not\equiv \frac{b}{d} \mod n\).
Solution
We can take \(a=10\), \(b=18\), \(c=2\), \(d=6\). Indeed,

text: Notice that here we’re using the test for non-divisibility from Example 3.2.6 .
html: Notice that here we’re using the test for non-divisibility from Example 3.2.6.
Lemma (squaring rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) . Then \(a^2\equiv b^2 \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\). Then \(a^2\equiv b^2 \mod n\).
Proof

text: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\) , there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\) . Then
html: Since \(a\equiv b \mod n\), there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(a-b=nx\). Then
Lemma (cubing rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) . Then \(a^3\equiv b^3 \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\). Then \(a^3\equiv b^3 \mod n\).

text: In fact the same is true for any power, although we don’t yet have the tools to prove it. We’ll come back to this later in the book, in Example 6.1.3 .
html: In fact the same is true for any power, although we don’t yet have the tools to prove it. We’ll come back to this later in the book, in Example 6.1.3.
Lemma (power rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(k\) be a natural number and let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) . Then \(a^k\equiv b^k \mod n\) .
html: Let \(k\) be a natural number and let \(a\), \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\). Then \(a^k\equiv b^k \mod n\).
Lemma (reflexivity rule for modular arithmetic)

text: Let \(a\) and \(n\) be integers. Then \(a\equiv a \mod n\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(n\) be integers. Then \(a\equiv a \mod n\).
Proof
\(a-a=n\cdot 0\), so \(n\mid a - a\).

text: Whew! That was a lot of lemmas. But they pay off, as you will now see. Suppose we now encounter some very specific modular arithmetic problem of the same general type as we’ve seen before: the congruence modulo \(n\) of two expressions which differ in a kind of spot-the-difference way, like a “rewriting” by one congruence. For example,
html: Whew! That was a lot of lemmas. But they pay off, as you will now see. Suppose we now encounter some very specific modular arithmetic problem of the same general type as we’ve seen before: the congruence modulo \(n\) of two expressions which differ in a kind of spot-the-difference way, like a “rewriting” by one congruence. For example,
Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv 2 \mod 4\) . Show that \(a b ^ 2 + a ^ 2 b + 3 a \equiv 2 b ^ 2 + 2 ^ 2 \cdot b + 3 \cdot 2 \mod 4\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv 2 \mod 4\). Show that \(a b ^ 2 + a ^ 2 b + 3 a \equiv 2 b ^ 2 + 2 ^ 2 \cdot b + 3 \cdot 2 \mod 4\).

text: We could solve this by working directly from the definition, which is rather painful:
html: We could solve this by working directly from the definition, which is rather painful:

text: Or, better, we can solve this by applying the right combination of the lemmas we already proved, in the right order. This requires much less thinking:
html: Or, better, we can solve this by applying the right combination of the lemmas we already proved, in the right order. This requires much less thinking:
Recall the problem from Example 3.3.11.
Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv 2 \mod 4\) . Show that \(a b ^ 2 + a ^ 2 b + 3 a \equiv 2 b ^ 2 + 2 ^ 2 \cdot b + 3 \cdot 2 \mod 4\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv 2 \mod 4\). Show that \(a b ^ 2 + a ^ 2 b + 3 a \equiv 2 b ^ 2 + 2 ^ 2 \cdot b + 3 \cdot 2 \mod 4\).

text: After solving this and the many similar problems in Section 3.3 , you probably feel like you can check the correctness of any statement of this style by sight. And that’s great! When we have built up enough theory that a particular class of statement can be checked by sight, we will stop requiring detailed proofs. So for now on we’ll take such statements for granted.
html: After solving this and the many similar problems in Section 3.3, you probably feel like you can check the correctness of any statement of this style by sight. And that’s great! When we have built up enough theory that a particular class of statement can be checked by sight, we will stop requiring detailed proofs. So for now on we’ll take such statements for granted.

text: This is also usually the point at which it’s easy to write a Lean tactic which checks the correctness of a class of statement. I’ve done exactly this, updating tactic rel to cover this kind of statement. We won’t discuss tactic-writing in this book, but effectively, the tactic now throws the lemmas Int.ModEq.add , Int.ModEq.neg , Int.ModEq.sub , Int.ModEq.mul , Int.ModEq.pow , Int.ModEq.refl and the provided hypotheses at the statement until (a) the goal is solved or (b) none of these lemmas apply any more. And that’s exactly what we’re doing in our heads when we check the paper statement of the problem.
html: This is also usually the point at which it’s easy to write a Lean tactic which checks the correctness of a class of statement. I’ve done exactly this, updating tactic rel to cover this kind of statement. We won’t discuss tactic-writing in this book, but effectively, the tactic now throws the lemmas Int.ModEq.add, Int.ModEq.neg, Int.ModEq.sub, Int.ModEq.mul, Int.ModEq.pow, Int.ModEq.refl and the provided hypotheses at the statement until (a) the goal is solved or (b) none of these lemmas apply any more. And that’s exactly what we’re doing in our heads when we check the paper statement of the problem.

text: From now on, we will solve more interesting modular arithmetic problems, dealing with steps like Example 3.3.11 in a single line.
html: From now on, we will solve more interesting modular arithmetic problems, dealing with steps like Example 3.3.11 in a single line.
Problem

text: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, with \(a \equiv 4\mod 5\) and \(b \equiv 3\mod 5\) . Show that \(ab+b^3+3 \equiv 2\mod 5\) .
html: Let \(a\) and \(b\) be integers, with \(a \equiv 4\mod 5\) and \(b \equiv 3\mod 5\). Show that \(ab+b^3+3 \equiv 2\mod 5\).
Solution
Problem

text: Show that there exists an integer \(a\) , such that \(6a \equiv 4\mod 11\) .
html: Show that there exists an integer \(a\), such that \(6a \equiv 4\mod 11\).
Solution

text: The integer 8 has this property. Indeed,
html: The integer 8 has this property. Indeed,
Problem

text: Let \(x\) be an integer. Show that \(x ^ 3 \equiv x\mod 3\) .
html: Let \(x\) be an integer. Show that \(x ^ 3 \equiv x\mod 3\).
Solution

text: We consider cases according to the residue of \(x\) modulo 3.
html: We consider cases according to the residue of \(x\) modulo 3.
Case 1 (\(x\equiv 0\mod 3\)):
Case 2 (\(x\equiv 1\mod 3\)):
Case 3 (\(x\equiv 2\mod 3\)):
Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer and suppose that \(5n\) is a multiple of \(8\) . Show that \(n\) is also a multiple of \(8\) .
html: Let \(n\) be an integer and suppose that \(5n\) is a multiple of \(8\). Show that \(n\) is also a multiple of \(8\).
Solution

text: Since \(8\mid 5n\) , there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(5n=8a\) . Then
html: Since \(8\mid 5n\), there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(5n=8a\). Then
so \(8\mid n\).

text: Such a problem will typically have many possible solutions. Here is another solution.
html: Such a problem will typically have many possible solutions. Here is another solution.
Solution

text: Since \(8\mid 5n\) , there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(5n=8a\) . Then
html: Since \(8\mid 5n\), there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(5n=8a\). Then
so \(8\mid n\).

text: Try typing the variant solution up in Lean.
html: Try typing the variant solution up in Lean.
Problem

text: Show that if for some integer \(n\) we have that \(5\) divides \(3n\) , then \(5\) also divides \(n\) .
html: Show that if for some integer \(n\) we have that \(5\) divides \(3n\), then \(5\) also divides \(n\).
Solution

text: Since \(5\mid 3n\) , there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(3n=5x\) . Then
html: Since \(5\mid 3n\), there exists an integer \(x\) such that \(3n=5x\). Then
so \(5\mid n\).
Problem

text: Let \(m\) be an integer which is divisible by 8 and by 5. Show that it is also divisible by 40.
html: Let \(m\) be an integer which is divisible by 8 and by 5. Show that it is also divisible by 40.
Solution

text: Since \(8\mid m\) , there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(m=8a\) . Since \(5\mid m\) , there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(m=5b\) . Then
html: Since \(8\mid m\), there exists an integer \(a\) such that \(m=8a\). Since \(5\mid m\), there exists an integer \(b\) such that \(m=5b\). Then
so \(40\mid m\).

• \(10-18=4\cdot -2\), so \(10\equiv 18 \mod 4\);
• \(2-6=4\cdot -1\), so \(2\equiv 6 \mod 4\);

text: \(\frac{10}{2}-\frac{18}{6}=2\) lies between the consecutive multiples \(4 \cdot 0\) and \(4 \cdot (0 + 1)\) of 4, so \(\frac{10}{2}\not\equiv \frac{18}{6} \mod 4\) .

text: (symmetry rule for modular arithmetic) Let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) . Show that \(b\equiv a \mod n\) . theorem Int.ModEq.symm ( h : a ≡ b [ ZMOD n ]) : b ≡ a [ ZMOD n ] := by sorry
html:
• (symmetry rule for modular arithmetic) Let \(a\), \(b\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\). Show that \(b\equiv a \mod n\).

    theorem Int.ModEq.symm (h : a ≡ b [ZMOD n]) : b ≡ a [ZMOD n] := by
  sorry

text: (transitivity rule for modular arithmetic) Let \(a\) , \(b\) , \(c\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(b\equiv c \mod n\) . Show that \(a\equiv c \mod n\) . theorem Int.ModEq.trans ( h1 : a ≡ b [ ZMOD n ]) ( h2 : b ≡ c [ ZMOD n ]) : a ≡ c [ ZMOD n ] := by sorry
html:
• (transitivity rule for modular arithmetic) Let \(a\), \(b\), \(c\) and \(n\) be integers, and suppose that \(a\equiv b \mod n\) and \(b\equiv c \mod n\). Show that \(a\equiv c \mod n\).

    theorem Int.ModEq.trans (h1 : a ≡ b [ZMOD n]) (h2 : b ≡ c [ZMOD n]) :
    a ≡ c [ZMOD n] := by
  sorry
